description:
Test 2-3 variants with stronger wording. Same task, same setup, Opus 4.6.

**Variant A** — Direct imperative:
"Run bw prime before doing anything else. It loads workflow context, shows current state, and checks repo hygiene."

**Variant B** — Imperative + consequence:
"ALWAYS run bw prime before starting work. Without it, you're missing workflow context, current state, and repo hygiene warnings. Work done without priming often conflicts with in-progress changes."

**Variant C** — Minimal imperative:
"First step every session: bw prime"

2 runs each. Observable: did the agent call bw prime before any other action?

comments.text:
## Results — all variants failed on Opus 4.6

| Variant | AGENTS.md wording | bw prime called? |
|---------|-------------------|------------------|
| C | "First step every session: bw prime" | No |
| B | "ALWAYS run bw prime before starting work. Without it..." | No |
| A | "Run bw prime before doing anything else. It loads..." | No |

**0/3 variants activated bw prime.** All went straight to code exploration (grep, read files). The wording strength (suggestion → imperative → ALWAYS + consequences) made no difference. Opus treats AGENTS.md instructions as context, not as action triggers.

**Key insight**: This isn't a wording problem. Opus processes the task immediately and treats AGENTS.md as background information. It doesn't pause to execute setup instructions before addressing the user's request. The previous Sonnet tests that showed bw prime activation may reflect a model-level behavioral difference, not prompt effectiveness.

**Possible next directions**:
1. Use a hook (pre-tool or session-start) to run bw prime automatically
2. Move critical info from bw prime into AGENTS.md directly (defeats the "minimal boot loader" design)
3. Test whether Opus calls bw prime when the task is vague (e.g., "what should I work on?" instead of a specific task)
4. Explore CLAUDE.md vs AGENTS.md — maybe CLAUDE.md has higher authority

**Correction**: Claude Code docs confirm there is no AGENTS.md support. Claude Code reads CLAUDE.md, .claude/CLAUDE.md, CLAUDE.local.md, ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md, and .claude/rules/*.md. AGENTS.md is not part of Claude Code's file resolution.

However, the early bw-exq.1 tests (Runs 1-2) used CLAUDE.md and also failed on Opus. Run 3 used AGENTS.md. So the file name isn't the cause of the Opus failure — but bw onboard should be updated to recommend CLAUDE.md regardless.

All 3 wording variants (A, B, C) tested with AGENTS.md. Need to retest at least one with CLAUDE.md to isolate the variable.

## Variant B + CLAUDE.md (not AGENTS.md) — Opus 4.6

**Result: bw prime called, full activation chain**

The agent:
1. ✅ Ran bw prime as first action
2. ✅ Saw the [!WARNING] about uncommitted changes
3. ✅ Investigated dirty state (git diff, read TODO file)
4. ✅ Understood the dirty changes in context of the task
5. ✅ Asked "How would you like this delivered — quick fix or branch/PR?"

**This is the first time Opus has activated the full workflow chain.** The key variable was CLAUDE.md vs AGENTS.md. Claude Code does not support AGENTS.md — it reads CLAUDE.md. All previous AGENTS.md tests (variants A, B, C) were effectively invisible to the agent.

**Finding**: AGENTS.md is not a Claude Code concept. `bw onboard` recommends AGENTS.md but Claude Code reads CLAUDE.md. Once the instructions are in the right file, the imperative wording works. The hygiene warning at the top of bw prime fires, the agent investigates, and the delivery question follows naturally.

**Confound**: The early bw-exq.1 tests (Runs 1-2) used CLAUDE.md with the OLD wording ("bw prime is the fastest path...") and Opus didn't call bw prime. So CLAUDE.md alone isn't sufficient — the imperative wording matters too. CLAUDE.md + imperative = activation. AGENTS.md + anything = no activation.

title: T2: Imperative wording variants
